AccomplishedActivities: [CLICK TRIP DETAIL BUTTON above to see more videos and details] - Today we all arrived in Athens. There are 5 from Texas, 5 from Wisconsin, and 2 from Colorado. It is a nice group. We gathered in our AirBNBs and then walked (and trained) to Mars Hill. There we took in the site and then gathered for devotional and communion. It was my pleasure to lead the conversation and communinion tonight.
GodMoments: We witnessed an incredible sunset on the hill. We also had open discussion about how so many of us have different faith traditionas, but we share so much in common with one another.
Challenges: A diverse of of people in age, occupation, geography, church demonination and backgrounds.
SharedPrayerRequests: That the team will come together and meld. We have some great opportunities ahead of us that will be even greater if we manage to bond quickly.

Pictures.Caption: Goodbye to my Little Piece of Heaven (Nafplio)
Still 45 minutes from Athens and the changes are evident. We are heading to big city.
The team is completely together. We journeyed to Mars Hill and I got to teach tonight as well as lead the team in communion. What a blessing.
The view of the Acroplis from Mars Hill. This was most likely the view that Paul had during his sermon.

PlannedActivities: Play soccer with Sudanese refugees in our socks. Share food and the gospel with them. Climb the Acropolis in Athens.
AccomplishedActivities: Today we traveled, by train, to the Sudanese Refugee encampment called Malakasa near Sfendali, Greece. It was about a 1 hour trip. This encampment is primarily male refugees looking for temporary or permenate EU passports (their papers). They live in shipping containers and have very little. We played Soccer (futbol) with them for 5 hours. They all have different financial means, which means some have shoes and some do not. So, everyone who played, had to play in only socks to level the playing field. It was a great day. In the morning we climbed the hill and stairs of the Acropolis to see the Parthenon and other Greek ruins.
GodMoments: Seeing the men play soccer together. It truly is beyond the language barrier. I am not sure there is anything on Earth more universal than Soccer/Football. Many say that love the international language (I don't disagree that we all experience love and can understand one another at the level), but Soccer is just as universal. All you need are 2 or more people, a bag, stuffing (sockes, cotton, weeds, trash, or a real ball), and two sticks and you can play soccer with someone and not be able to speak a single shared word. (Except everyone seems to know how to call for the ball to be passed to them. LOL)
Challenges: It was warm on the pitch. Several of the guys got blisters. Always wanting to help people more.
SharedPrayerRequests: That God opens the heart of the men we met today to know His son, our Lord and Savior Jesus.

Pictures.Caption: Our team from Wisconsin, Colorado, and Texas. Back Row (left to right): Ellen, Brent, Brent, Josh, Hannah, Zoey, Matt and Bryan. Front Row: Michael, Amelia, and Emma
View of Athens walking up to the Acropolis
The ancient Roman theater built in 161 AD named Odeon of Herodes Atticus. This theater is still used today. If you look close, you can see people working to set up a concernt for tonight.
A view of the back side of the theater with the Parthenon looming over head.

Some kind of fruit that was picked from a tree near where we played soccer today. One of the locals picked it and put it in his hat and handed it out to us. It was delicious. If you know what it was, please let me know.
Some of the Sudanese Refugees listening to instructions on how we are going to play today, along with Josh and Matt from the team.

PlannedActivities: We will meet with children (homeless and refugee) and just play games with them. Show them the love of Christ through games. We will feed them at the end. In the evening we are going on a Homeless walk. The is where Arturo leads us to visit many homeless in our area. We hand out sandwiches, juice boxes, and other items. I will say that this activity literallly and obsolutely changed my life last year. Look for my sermon entitled The Least of These where I talk about the lady and the Red Juice Box.
AccomplishedActivities: We had a fantastic day, but it of course came with its challenges. First, we needed to be at Stavros Niarchos Park at 10 AM. The plan was to the Metro train at 9 AM, which we did. As I started investigating, it came to my attention that the 911 bus we needed to take at the end of the train ride does not run on Saturday mornings. So, the options were to take Taxi's, to take multiple buses, or to walk. We collectively decided to walk the 2.7 KM to the park. It was a nice walk and it was early in the morning, so we were still fresh. We met the kids, teens, young adults, and parents of the Porta Albanian Church of Athens. We played kickball, soccer, tag, frisbee, and many other games for the next 4 hours. We ate pizza together and then decided to walk and tour the park and nearby Library. At this point we already had 14,000 steps and getting tired. We walked to the library and then a local who was with us said no problem we will go to the tram to get back to the train station. BTW, it was hot. We walked to the Tram and waited 12 minutes for the tram. It came and was completely packed with people. The locals on the tram had no interest in letting us on and I am not sure we could have put the 10 of us on it. So, we decided to walk the 2 miles back to the train station. Did I mention it was hot. We walked for the next hour and finally got on the train. We made it back to the apartment and Arturo, from the homeless ministry, met us there to guide us on the homeless evening walk. (That is where we take sandwiches to homeless people sleeping on the streets.) So, we walked for another 2 hours. Needless to say, we walked 30,000+ steps.
GodMoments: The Albanians were such fun and we loved on them and enjoyed this time and serving. The homeless walk touched our hearts. We handed out 75 sandwiches and candy bars. One group specifically reallly touched our hearts. They was a dad and 2 kids. They were on a busy downtown sidewalk/street. The dad was leaning up against the wall on a very hot pavement. (It was 7:45 PM). In his arms was what appeared to be a 4 year child and laying on the pavement next to him was another child about 6 or 7. They were completely asleep. At first our hearts were broken to see this and as Pastor Michael quoted, God, this should not be. All of us cried, but then God spoke straight into me. He instantly showed me the beauty of this family embracing and meeting each others needs. I found myself being stuck inbetween the world of dispair and joy at the same moment. It is so difficult to explain how you can weep for someone and find joy in them at the same time. THANK YOU GOD!!!
Challenges: Sore feet!!! LOL - But really being here in the moment, serving others, and keeping the mentality that just because we are here to help and serve, it does not make us better than those we are serving. As we humble ourselves to serve, we sometimes get puffed up and lose humility. Then superiority kicks in and we place ourselves as better than them. It is at this point that we realize we are THE LEAST of THESE as well.
SharedPrayerRequests: Continued growth, good sleep, hearts to serve and to see people who so desperately want to be seen.

After our walk, we had dinner in the beautiful restaurant. Everyone of us felt very odd to be eating like this when we were just on the streets giving out sandwiches to people and we are not in this space.
This is an image of a man (father) with his 4 year old and 6 year old children. We all wept at the sight, but I was able to find the beauty in a father protecting his children.
Our team with the Albanian kids.

PlannedActivities: Attend a local church service and Homeless Walk
AccomplishedActivities: Today we went to church and worshipped with the Albanians members of La Porta Church in Athens, Greece. We sang and learned and took communion and sang some more and fellowshipped and ate a great meal. We love the Albanian Church and its lovely members. After church we came back to our apartment and had a really nice debrief time with the entire team. A lot of us opened up, some of us cried, we laughed, and got to know each other better. We did our devotional after the debrief time and we realized that our open debrief discussion actually covered the topics of the devo. God is truely GOOD!!! After devo we made more sandwiches and went on another Homeless Walk. We saw some of the same people and some new ones. Last night, I had the pleasure to meet John from England. He and I talked for about 15 minutes. He shared a lot about himself and I was able to pray with him. I was not supposed to go on the homeless walk again tonight, but I wanted to see John again. I wanted him to know that someone wanted to come see him again. I actually got seperated from the team, but I found John on my own. I sat and talked with him for over 45 minutes. He shared so many things about himself and about his journey. He shared some very personal and intimate details. I was so blessed by our time. When I finally had to leave, he asked me to come back if I can, but he understood if I could not. I told him to take care of himself. He said, taking care of himself is easy, the loneliness is not!!! What an image into the lives of the homeless and lost refugees in Athens Greece.
GodMoments: The worship this morning, the love of that church. And the time I spent with John. Also coming together as a team in our debrief and devotional time today.
Challenges: Energy and not being able to help as much as we wish we could and as much as the people of Athens need us.
SharedPrayerRequests: Please pray for our trip to Corinth tomorrow and for my new friend, John in the Park.
